title: Override inspect in Combined, Env & Encrypted Configurations

body: Don't think we need to expose this as a public method. Can just do it in the inspect.

title: Override inspect in Combined, Env & Encrypted Configurations
diff_hunk:
@@ -130,7 +130,14 @@ def config

     end
 
+    # Returns an array of all configuration keys.

+    end
+
     def inspect # :nodoc:
+      "#<#{self.class.name}:#{'%#016x' % (object_id << 1)} keys=#{keys.inspect}>"
+    rescue
       "#<#{self.class.name}:#{'%#016x' % (object_id << 1)}>"
body: What are we rescuing?

title: Override inspect in Combined, Env & Encrypted Configurations
diff_hunk:
@@ -1,22 +1,30 @@
-*   Override `inspect` methods in `CombinedConfiguration` and `EnvConfiguration` to prevent
-    sensitive ENV variables and credentials from being exposed in console or logs.
-    This matches the existing security behavior of `EncryptedConfiguration`.
+*   Override `inspect` methods in `CombinedConfiguration`, `EnvConfiguration`, and
+    `EncryptedConfiguration` to show key names while hiding sensitive values. This prevents
+    accidental exposure of secrets in console or logs while still providing useful debugging
+    information about what configuration keys are available.
 
     Before:
 
     ```ruby
+    Rails.app.creds
     #<ActiveSupport::CombinedConfiguration:0x0000000122b45870
      @configurations=[
        #<ActiveSupport::EnvConfiguration:0x0000000122b465b8
-        @envs={"SECRET" => "SECRET_MSG", ... }>,
+        @envs={"SOME_SECRET" =>  "secret_value", ...}>,
        #<ActiveSupport::EncryptedConfiguration:0x00000000007af0>]>
     ```
 
     After:
 
     ```ruby
     Rails.app.creds
-    => #<ActiveSupport::CombinedConfiguration:0x0000000122b45870>
+    #<ActiveSupport::CombinedConfiguration:0x00000000006490 keys=["RAILS_ENV", "SOME_SECRET", ..., :secret_key_base, :stripe_webhook_secret]>
body: We should be converting all these keys to symbols, since that's how you're going to access them.

title: Override inspect in Combined, Env & Encrypted Configurations
diff_hunk:
@@ -50,5 +50,16 @@ def option(*key, default: nil)
     def reload
       @configurations.each { |config| config.try(:reload) }
     end
+
+    def inspect # :nodoc:
+      keys = @configurations.flat_map { |config|
+        if config.is_a?(ActiveSupport::EnvConfiguration)
+          config.instance_variable_get(:@envs).keys.map { |k| k.downcase.to_sym }
+        elsif config.is_a?(ActiveSupport::EncryptedConfiguration)
+          config.config.keys
+        end
+      }.compact.uniq
body: Hmm, this is indeed rather messy. Probably fine to add #keys back just to provide this in a cleaner manner, so you can do `@configurations.flat_map(&:keys).uniq`.

title: Override inspect in Combined, Env & Encrypted Configurations
diff_hunk:
@@ -1,3 +1,34 @@
+*   Override `inspect` methods in `CombinedConfiguration`, `EnvConfiguration`, and
+    `EncryptedConfiguration` to show key names while hiding sensitive values. This prevents
+    accidental exposure of secrets in console or logs while still providing useful debugging
+    information about what configuration keys are available.
+
+    Before:
+
+    ```ruby
+    Rails.app.creds
+    #<ActiveSupport::CombinedConfiguration:0x0000000122b45870
+     @configurations=[
+       #<ActiveSupport::EnvConfiguration:0x0000000122b465b8
+        @envs={"SOME_SECRET" =>  "secret_value", ...}>,
+       #<ActiveSupport::EncryptedConfiguration:0x00000000007af0>]>
+    ```
+
+    After:
+
+    ```ruby
+    Rails.app.creds
+    #<ActiveSupport::CombinedConfiguration:0x00000000006490 keys=[:rails_env, :some_secret, ..., :secret_key_base, :stripe_webhook_secret]>
+
+    Rails.app.credentials
+    #<ActiveSupport::EncryptedConfiguration:0x00000000008cd0 keys=[:secret_key_base, :stripe_webhook_secret]>
+
+    Rails.app.envs
+    #<ActiveSupport::EnvConfiguration:0x0000000000a6a0 keys=[:rails_env, :some_secret, :path, :home, ...]>
+    ```
+
+    *Emmanuel Hayford*
body: Since this is an unreleased feature, we don't need a CHANGELOG entry. But we can amend the other entries to note that these new configuration objects are inspect safe.

title: Override inspect in Combined, Env & Encrypted Configurations
diff_hunk:
@@ -53,6 +53,25 @@ def reload
       @envs = ENV.to_h
     end
 
+    # Returns an array of symbolized keys from the environment variables.
+    #
+    # Examples:
+    #
+    #   ENV["DB_HOST"] = "localhost"
+    #   ENV["DATABASE__PORT"] = "5432"
+    #
+    #   env_config = ActiveSupport::EnvConfiguration.new
+    #   env_config.keys
+    #   # => [:db_host, :database__port, ...]
body: `:database__port` is not a valid key, actually. The keys are nested, so it would need to be presented as `database: [ :port ]` or the like. Not entirely trivial, but we can't present keys that aren't correct either.

title: Fix & standardize comments for CombinedConfiguration
diff_hunk:
@@ -13,7 +13,8 @@ def initialize(*configurations)
       @configurations = configurations
     end
 
-    # Find singular or nested keys across all backends. If no backend holds the key, it raises +KeyError+.
+    # Find singular or nested keys across all backends.
+    # Raises +KeyError+ if no backend holds the key or if the value is nil.
body: I don't think nil should be respected, no. We should search through all the configurations for a not-nil value. If we can't find one, we raise MissingKey.

title: Fix & standardize comments for CombinedConfiguration
diff_hunk:

       @options = nil
     end
 
-    # Find the referenced key
-    # Raises +KeyError+ if not found.
+    # Find singular or nested keys.
+    # Raises +KeyError+ if not found or nil.
     #
     # Examples:
     #
-    #   require(:db_host)         # => ENV.fetch("DB_HOST")
-    #   require(:database, :host) # => ENV.fetch("DATABASE__HOST")
+    #   require(:db_host)         # => dig(:db_host) || raise(KeyError)
+    #   require(:database, :host) # => dig(:database, :host) || raise(KeyError)

title: Add Rails.app.creds to provide combined credentials lookup in ENV and encrypted file
diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,58 @@
+# frozen_string_literal: true
+
+# require "active_support/core_ext/module/delegation"
+
+module ActiveSupport
+  # Provide a better interface for accessing configuration options stored in ENV.
+  # Keys are accepted as symbols and turned into upcased strings. Nesting is provided by double underscores.
+  #
+  # This interface mirrors what is used for +ActiveSupport::EncryptedConfiguration+ and thus allows both
+  # to serve as interchangeable backends for +Rails.app.credentials+.
+  #
+  # Examples:
+  #
+  #   config = EnvConfiguration.new
+  #   config[:db_host] # => ENV["DB_HOST"]
+  #   config.dig(:database, :host) # => ENV["DATABASE__HOST"]
+  class EnvConfiguration
+    # Combined interface for #[] and #dig. Examples:
+    #
+    #   creds.grab(:db_host) # => creds[:db_host]
+    #   creds.grab(:database, :host) # => creds.dig(:database, :host)
+    def grab(*keys)
+      if keys.many?
+        dig(*keys)
+      else
+        self[keys.first]
+      end
+    end
+
+    # Find a upcased string-version of the +key+ in ENV.
+    #
+    # Example:
+    #
+    #   config[:db_host] # => ENV["DB_HOST"]
+    def [](key)
+      lookup envify(key)
+    end
+
+    # Find a upcased and double-underscored-joined string-version of the +keys+ in ENV.
+    #
+    # Examples:
+    #
+    #   config.dig(:db_host) # => ENV["DB_HOST"]
+    #   config.dig(:database, :host) # => ENV["DATABASE__HOST"]
+    def dig(*keys)
+      lookup(Array(keys).collect { |key| envify(key) }.join("__"))
+    end
+
+    private
+      def lookup(env_key)
+        ENV[env_key]
body: I like that idea. Think it's totally fine to cache upfront too with a #reload. This is almost always about having these values set at boot time anyway.

title: Add Rails.app.creds to provide combined credentials lookup in ENV and encrypted file
diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,45 @@
+# frozen_string_literal: true
+
+require "active_support/core_ext/enumerable"
+
+module ActiveSupport
+  # Allows for configuration keys to be pulled from multiple backends. Keys are pulled in first-found order from
+  # the configuration backends that the combined configuration has been initialized with.
+  #
+  # This is used by Rails to offer a unified API for fetching credentials from both ENV and the encrypted file.
+  class CombinedConfiguration
+    def initialize(*configurations)
+      @configurations = configurations
+    end
+
+    # Combined interface for #[] and #dig. Examples:
+    #
+    #   creds.grab(:db_host) # => creds[:db_host]
+    #   creds.grab(:database, :host) # => creds.dig(:database, :host)
+    def grab(*keys)
body: Yes, we should. I was thinking it was either dig for multiple or [] for single, but of course you can just use dig for both.

title: Add Rails.app.creds to provide combined credentials lookup in ENV and encrypted file
diff_hunk:
@@ -58,6 +58,42 @@ def initialize(config_path:, key_path:, env_key:, raise_if_missing_key:)
       @options = nil
     end
 
+    # Find the referenced key
+    # Raises +KeyError+ if not found.
+    #
+    # Examples:
+    #
+    #   require(:db_host)         # => ENV.fetch("DB_HOST")
+    #   require(:database, :host) # => ENV.fetch("DATABASE__HOST")
+    def require(*key)
+      dig(*key) || raise(KeyError)
+    end
+
+    # Find a upcased and double-underscored-joined string-version of the +keys+ in ENV.
+    # Returns nil if the key isn't found or the value of default when passed If default is
+    # a block, it's called first.
+    #
+    # Examples:
+    #
+    #   config.option(:db_host)                                    # => ENV["DB_HOST"]
+    #   config.option(:database, :host)                            # => ENV["DATABASE__HOST"]
+    #   config.option(:database, :host, default: "missing")        # => ENV.fetch("DATABASE__HOST", "missing")
+    #   config.option(:database, :host, default: -> { "missing" }) # => ENV.fetch("DATABASE__HOST", default.call)
body: I considered this, as it would match fetch, but never liked the unexplained block. And since we're doing a totally new API here with require/option, I prefer to be explicit about the default. Like we do in other places, like attributes.

title: Add Rails.app.creds to provide combined credentials lookup in ENV and encrypted file
diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,56 @@
+# frozen_string_literal: true
+
+require "active_support/core_ext/enumerable"
+
+module ActiveSupport
+  # Allows for configuration keys to be pulled from multiple backends. Keys are pulled in first-found order from
+  # the configuration backends that the combined configuration has been initialized with.
+  #
+  # This is used by Rails to offer a unified API for fetching credentials from both ENV and the encrypted file.
+  # You can access this through +Rails.app.creds+ within a Rails application.
+  class CombinedConfiguration
+    def initialize(*configurations)
+      @configurations = configurations
body:
That's a good idea, but I think in most cases, you'd want this inserted after the ENVs lookups. So you can still overwrite through that. So I've added `Rails.app.creds=`, which can take a custom `ActiveSupport::CombinedConfiguration` instance. I've also split out `Rails.app.envs`, so you could do:

```ruby
Rails.app.creds = ActiveSupport::CombinedConfiguration.new \
  Rails.app.envs, EjsonConfiguration.new
```

title: Add Rails.app.creds to provide combined credentials lookup in ENV and encrypted file

     end

+    #
+    # Examples:
+    #
+    #   require(:db_host)         # => ENV.fetch("DB_HOST")
+    #   require(:database, :host) # => ENV.fetch("DATABASE__HOST")
+    def require(*key)
+      dig(*key) || raise(KeyError)
body: Ah yes. What an awkward implementation that gives, though. Wish Ruby had an || like operator for this purpose. |? or whatever.

title: Add Rails.app.creds to provide combined credentials lookup in ENV and encrypted file
diff_hunk:
@@ -58,6 +59,48 @@ def initialize(config_path:, key_path:, env_key:, raise_if_missing_key:)
       @options = nil
     end
 
+    # Find the referenced key
+    # Raises +KeyError+ if not found.
+    #
+    # Examples:
+    #
+    #   require(:db_host)         # => ENV.fetch("DB_HOST")
+    #   require(:database, :host) # => ENV.fetch("DATABASE__HOST")
+    def require(*key)
+      value = dig(*key)
+
+      if value.defined?
+        value
+      else
+        raise(KeyError)
+      end
body:
I've wanted this for a long time. Just like we have present/blank. Every existential predicate should come in a positive and a negative. This goes right to Ruby's if/unless insight. So this is just riding along with this PR, because it highlighted it.

Also, I really dislike guard statements inside methods that produce multi exit wounds.

title: Add a summary report at the end of `ContinousIntegration.run`
diff_hunk:
@@ -123,13 +123,25 @@ def report(title, &block)
         echo "\n✅ #{title} passed in #{elapsed}", type: :success
       else
         echo "\n❌ #{title} failed in #{elapsed}", type: :error
+        ci.failure_summary(title)
       end
 
       results.concat ci.results
     ensure
       Signal.trap("INT", "-")
     end
 
+    # :nodoc:
+    def failure_summary(title)
+      return if success? || results.size < 2
body: We're already on the failure branch of the former conditional, so think we can drop `success?` and I also think we should probably extract `results.size < 2` as `multiple_steps?` and put that into the caller, so it's `ci.failure_summary(title) if multiple_steps?`, so it's clear where the presentation is being made whether it needs a summary.

title: Add a summary report at the end of `ContinousIntegration.run`
diff_hunk:
@@ -123,13 +123,25 @@ def report(title, &block)
         echo "\n✅ #{title} passed in #{elapsed}", type: :success
       else
         echo "\n❌ #{title} failed in #{elapsed}", type: :error
+        ci.failure_summary(title)
body:
Actually, I would inline the production of the output here like this:

```ruby
def report(title, &block)
  Signal.trap("INT") { abort colorize(:error, "\n❌ #{title} interrupted") }

  ci = self.class.new
  elapsed = timing { ci.instance_eval(&block) }

  if ci.success?
    echo "\n✅ #{title} passed in #{elapsed}", type: :success
  else
    echo "\n❌ #{title} failed in #{elapsed}", type: :error

    failures.each do |title|
      echo "   ↳ #{title} failed", type: :error
    end
  end

  results.concat ci.results
ensure
  Signal.trap("INT", "-")
end

def failures
  results.select(&:first)
end
```

title: Add assert_in_body/assert_not_in_body
diff_hunk:
@@ -71,6 +71,20 @@ def assert_redirected_to(url_options = {}, options = {}, message = nil)
         assert_operator redirect_expected, :===, redirect_is, message
       end
 
+      # Asserts that the given +text+ is present somewhere in the response body.
+      #
+      #     assert_in_body fixture(:name).description
+      def assert_in_body(text)
+        assert_match(/#{text}/, @response.body)

title: Structured CI with bin/ci
diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,58 @@
+module ActiveSupport
+  class ContinuousIntegration
+    COLORS = {
+      banner: "\033[1;32m",   # Green
+      title: "\033[1;35m",    # Purple
+      subtitle: "\033[1;90m", # Medium Gray
+      error: "\033[1;31m",    # Red
+      success: "\033[1;32m"   # Green
+    }
+

+    end
+
+    def initialize
+    end
+

+

body: Added the option for multi-arg system calls, but find the syntax cumbersome when it's not adding an escaping benefit, so think we'll just leave the default ci DSL as-is. But now people can at least take advantage of the escaping if they need it. Good suggestion!

title: Structured CI with bin/ci
diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,145 @@
+# frozen_string_literal: true
+
+module ActiveSupport
+  # Provides a DSL for declaring a continuous integration workflow that can be run either locally or in the cloud.
+  # Each step is timed, reports success/error, and is aggregated into a collective report that reports total runtime,
+  # as well as whether the entire run was successful or not.
+  #
+  # Example:
+  #
+  #   ActiveSupport::ContinuousIntegration.run do
+  #     step "Setup", "bin/setup --skip-server"
+  #     step "Style: Ruby", "bin/rubocop"
+  #     step "Security: Gem audit", "bin/bundler-audit"
+  #     step "Tests: Rails", "bin/rails test test:system"
+  #
+  #     if success?
+  #       step "Signoff: Ready for merge and deploy", "gh signoff"
+  #     else
+  #       failure "Skipping signoff; CI failed.", "Fix the issues and try again."
+  #     end
+  #   end
+  #
+  # Starting with Rails 8.1, a default `bin/ci` and `config/ci.rb` file are created to provide out-of-the-box CI.
+  class ContinuousIntegration
+    COLORS = {
+      banner: "\033[1;32m",   # Green
+      title: "\033[1;35m",    # Purple
+      subtitle: "\033[1;90m", # Medium Gray
+      error: "\033[1;31m",    # Red
+      success: "\033[1;32m"   # Green
+    }
+
+    attr_reader :results
+
+    # Perform a CI run. Execute each step, show their results and runtime, and exit with a non-zero status if there are any failures.
+    #
+    # Pass an optional title, subtitle, and a block that declares the steps to be executed.
+    #
+    # Sets the CI environment variable to "true" to allow for conditional behavior in the app, like enabling eager loading and disabling logging.
+    #
+    # Example:
+    #
+    #   ActiveSupport::ContinuousIntegration.run do
+    #     step "Setup", "bin/setup --skip-server"
+    #     step "Style: Ruby", "bin/rubocop"
+    #     step "Security: Gem audit", "bin/bundler-audit"
+    #     step "Tests: Rails", "bin/rails test test:system"
+    #
+    #     if success?
+    #       step "Signoff: Ready for merge and deploy", "gh signoff"
+    #     else
+    #       failure "Skipping signoff; CI failed.", "Fix the issues and try again."
+    #     end
+    #   end
+    def self.run(title = "Continuous Integration", subtitle = "Running tests, style checks, and security audits", &block)
+      new.tap do |ci|
+        ENV["CI"] = "true"
+        ci.heading title, subtitle, padding: false
+        ci.report(title, &block)
+        abort unless ci.success?
+      end
+    end
+
+    def initialize(&block)
body: Good catch. Removed in f98c0188ae2e.

title: Structured CI with bin/ci
diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,33 @@
+# Run using bin/ci
+
+CI.run do
+  step "Setup", "bin/setup --skip-server"
+<% unless options.skip_rubocop? %>
+  step "Style: Ruby", "bin/rubocop"
+<% end -%>
+
+  step "Security: Gem audit", "bin/bundler-audit"
+<% if using_node? -%>
+  step "Security: Yarn vulnerability audit", "yarn audit"
+<% end -%>
+<% if options[:javascript] == "importmap" && !options[:api] && !options[:skip_javascript] -%>
+  step "Security: Importmap vulnerability audit", "bin/importmap audit"
+<% end -%>
+<% unless options.skip_brakeman? -%>
+  step "Security: Brakeman code analysis", "bin/brakeman --quiet --no-pager --exit-on-warn --exit-on-error"
+<% end -%>
+<% if options[:api] || options[:skip_system_test] -%>
+  step "Tests: Rails", "bin/rails test"
+<% else %>
+  step "Tests: Rails", "bin/rails test test:system"
body: No, I think test:all mixes all tests together. We want to run all the regular tests first, then the system tests. But we can also just split it into two separate steps?

title: Authenticate the action cable connection too
diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,17 @@
+module ApplicationCable
+  class Connection < ActionCable::Connection::Base
+    identified_by :current_user
+
+    def connect
+      set_current_user || reject_unauthorized_connection
+    end
+
+    private
+      def set_current_user
+        if session = Session.find_by(id: cookies.signed[:session_id])
+          self.current_user = session.user
+        end
body: I don't think that makes sense here given that this connection is assuming that everyone needs to be authenticated or be rejected. Therefore the rejection is essentially an exceptional state. You shouldn't be trying to establish a connection unless you believe it would succeed. So this complicates the code for a condition that isn't worth optimizing for.

title: Silence healthcheck requests from the log
diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,37 @@
+# frozen_string_literal: true
+
+# :markup: markdown
+
+require "active_support/logger_silence"
+
+module Rails
+  module Rack
+    # Allows you to silence requests made to a specific path.
+    # This is useful for preventing recurring requests like healthchecks from clogging the logging.
+    # This middleware is used to do just that against the path /up in production by default.
+    #
+    # Example:
+    #
+    #   config.middleware.insert_before \
+    #     Rails::Rack::Logger, Rails::Rack::SilenceRequest, path: "/up"
+    #
+    # This middleware can also be configured using `config.silence_healthcheck = "/up"` in Rails.
+    class SilenceRequest
+      def initialize(app, path:)
+        @app, @path = app, path
+      end
+
+      def call(env)
+        request = ActionDispatch::Request.new(env)
+
+        if request.path == @path
body: I had that at first, but I then arrested myself with a charge of YAGNI. I couldn't come up with any other use cases except for the healthcheck, so decided to focus on that. The API is a lot more straight forward than something generic.

title: bundle update rubocop --conservative

title: bundle update rubocop --conservative

title: Allow overriding SQLite defaults from `database.yml`
diff_hunk:
@@ -759,29 +759,40 @@ def configure_connection
 
           super
 
+          pragmas = @config.fetch(:pragmas, {}).stringify_keys
+
           # Enforce foreign key constraints
           # https://www.sqlite.org/pragma.html#pragma_foreign_keys
           # https://www.sqlite.org/foreignkeys.html
-          raw_execute("PRAGMA foreign_keys = ON", "SCHEMA")
+          @raw_connection.foreign_keys = pragmas.fetch("foreign_keys", true)
           unless @memory_database
             # Journal mode WAL allows for greater concurrency (many readers + one writer)
             # https://www.sqlite.org/pragma.html#pragma_journal_mode
-            raw_execute("PRAGMA journal_mode = WAL", "SCHEMA")
+            @raw_connection.journal_mode = pragmas.fetch("journal_mode", :wal)
body: Fair discussion, but I concur that WAL should be the default. We intend for sqlite to be a full option for use as the main application database, and this is a better option for this.

title: Ensure SQLite transaction default to IMMEDIATE mode
diff_hunk:
@@ -358,7 +358,7 @@ def with_transaction_returning_status
       connection = self.class.connection
       ensure_finalize = !connection.transaction_open?
 
-      connection.transaction do
+      connection.transaction(mode: :immediate) do
body: Don't want to have a SQLite3 specific mode concern leak all the way into the other adapters and the root transaction setup. There's gotta be a better way with less collateral damage.
